brief_justification: Arrives at the exact correct answer (Yes) with valid compounding-pathway logic; traces second and third-order effects through two independent causal chains and notes they amplify rather than offset; shows some reasoning awareness ('the framing implies reversal — it doesn't') but does not name its own biases explicitly; applies an explicit counterfactual boundary test (mice-as-dispersers falsification); acknowledges uncertainty implicitly through the falsification frame but lacks structured confidence levels; identifies and explicitly rejects the reversal alternative with a specific boundary condition; every derivation step is shown with a diagram and table enabling full reproduction; avoids the 'intervention improves health' anti-pattern by concluding decline, but does not name the negative gate explicitly; avoids most suppressed failure modes (no single-chain thinking, no static-system assumption) but doesn't explicitly call out the suppressed terms; constructs a parallel-pathway structured reasoning but does not follow the injected topology's gate/loop/M-node structure closely.
